title: What a Service Mesh Does
description:
## Service-to-Service Communication

Your microservices need mutual TLS, automatic retries, traffic splitting for canary releases, and per-call telemetry — ideally **without rewriting application code**. Which best describes a **service mesh** (e.g. Istio, Linkerd, Consul, built on proxies like Envoy)?

options.text: A dedicated infrastructure layer — usually injected as **sidecar proxies** next to each service — that manages service-to-service traffic, enforces **mutual TLS**, and adds retries, traffic management and observability transparently to the app
A CI/CD tool that builds and deploys your microservices from source
A distributed database that the microservices use to store their shared state
A drop-in replacement for your container runtime (containerd / Docker) on each node

explanation: A **service mesh** is a dedicated **infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication**. It typically deploys a **sidecar proxy** (commonly Envoy) alongside each service instance; all traffic flows through these proxies, which transparently provide **mutual TLS** encryption/identity, **retries**, timeouts, **traffic management** (canary/blue-green splitting), and rich **observability** — all **without changing application code**. Istio, Linkerd and Consul are mesh implementations. It is not a CI/CD tool (b), not a database (c), and not a container runtime (d) — those are entirely different layers of the stack.

hints.title: It's a networking layer, not the app
hints.body: The defining trait is the sidecar proxy intercepting traffic to handle mTLS, retries, routing and telemetry — so developers get these features for free without touching their service code.
